text: The patient is positioned in a modified Lloyd-Davies position with a bladder catheter inserted. The surgical setup includes a 10mm subarm ligoport and two 5mm ports on each side of the abdomen. The surgeon retracts the uterus to visualize the area of intrarectal interception and places transabdominal proline sutures to retract the bulky uterus.

The surgeon makes a peritoneal incision over the sacral promontory, extending it distally in an inverted J shape along the right side of the rectum and over the deepest part of the pouch of Douglas. An anterior-only rectal mobilization is performed to minimize the risk of damage to the lateral neurovascular pedicles.

The surgeon performs a posterior dissection limited to the sacral promontory, using a malleable retractor in the vagina to delineate the vaginal wall. The rectovaginal septum is incised and dissected down to the pelvic floor, approximately two to three centimeters above the dentate line. A proline mesh, trimmed to an L-shaped configuration, is secured to the pelvic floor muscles on either side of the upper anal canal using tackers, avoiding direct placement into the rectal wall. The mesh is anchored to the sacral promontory with Etibon II-O sutures.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum over the mesh using V-lock sutures, completing the procedure with the mesh securely positioned beneath the peritoneal layer.
